name: Ruta de las Ermitas – circular from Lagunilla de Jubera by Leza de Río Leza
name_original: Ruta de las Ermitas – circular desde Lagunilla de Jubera por Leza de Río Leza
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Lagunilla Del Jubera offer diverse landscapes in the La Rioja region of Spain. The area features varied topography, from dry grasslands and mountains with sparse woodlands to the fertile agricultural lands of the Jubera valley. Cyclists can experience diverse elevations, including the higher reaches of the Zenzano mountains and the dramatic Leza Canyon. The Salado River also meanders through the region, contributing to the natural scenery. Best touring cycling routes around Lagunilla Del Jubera The most popular touring cycling route is Camero Viejo Loop from Ribafrecha, a 34.9 miles (56.2 km) trail that takes 4 hours 27 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the varied terrain of the Camero Viejo mountains. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Ruta de las Ermitas – circular from Lagunilla de Jubera by Leza de Río Leza, a difficult 16.6 miles (26.8 km) path. This route passes by ancient hermitages and offers views of the Jubera valley. Local touring cyclists also love the Arnedillo and thermal baths – Cidacos Greenway loop from Santa Engracia del Jubera, a 31.2 miles (50.1 km) trail leading through the Cidacos Greenway, often completed in about 4 hours 53 minutes. Touring cycling around Lagunilla Del Jubera is defined by dry grasslands, mountains with sparse woodlands, and intriguing rock formations. The network offers options for various ability levels, including 1 easy, 4 moderate, and 9 difficult routes. The routes in Lagunilla Del Jubera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from 3 reviews. More than 160 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Lagunilla Del Jubera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Lagunilla Del Jubera?
A: There are 14 touring cycling routes around Lagunilla Del Jubera. These routes offer a mix of difficulties, including 1 easy, 4 moderate, and 9 difficult options, ensuring there's something for every skill level.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Lagunilla Del Jubera?
A: The region offers diverse topography, from dry grasslands and mountains with sparse woodlands to the fertile agricultural lands of the Jubera valley. You'll encounter varied elevations, including the higher reaches of the Zenzano mountains and the dramatic Leza Canyon. The Salado River also meanders through the area, adding to the natural scenery.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, there is 1 easy touring cycling route available. While many routes are more challenging, the area's varied terrain means that hybrid bikes are often recommended, allowing riders to comfortably navigate both paved roads and gravel trails. For a less strenuous option, consider exploring the agricultural lands of the Jubera valley.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, several routes are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994449613/ruta-de-las-ermitas-circular-from-lagunilla-de-jubera-by-leza-de-rio-leza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruta de las Ermitas – circular from Lagunilla de Jubera by Leza de Río Leza&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 16.6-mile path that passes by ancient hermitages. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38153531" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arnedillo and thermal baths – Cidacos Greenway loop from Santa Engracia del Jubera&lt;/a&gt;, a 31.2-mile trail through the Cidacos Greenway.

Q: What historical sites or natural landmarks can I see along the routes?
A: The region is rich in historical and natural attractions. You can encounter ancient hermitages like the Hermita de Villavieja and the Romanesque Ermita de Nuestra Señora del Manzo. The 16th-century parish church of San Andrés is also a national historical monument. For natural sights, look out for the stunning Leza Canyon, the Salado River, and intriguing rock formations. You might even spot a large colony of vultures nesting in the rocks. Specific highlights include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2706737" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Clavijo Castle&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3753514" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Leza Canyon Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Lagunilla Del Jubera?
A: La Rioja generally offers good cycling conditions for much of the year. The dry grasslands and sparse woodlands suggest pleasant riding during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be warm, but the varied elevations mean you can find cooler spots. Winter conditions might be challenging in the higher mountain areas.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists?
A: Yes, there are 9 difficult routes for experienced touring cyclists. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e994478593/camero-viejo-loop-from-ribafrecha" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camero Viejo Loop from Ribafrecha&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 34.9-mile trail exploring the varied terrain of the Camero Viejo mountains. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38193665" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Murillo de Río Leza – Ventas Blancas loop from Agoncillo&lt;/a&gt;, a 71.4 km route with significant elevation changes.

Q: What do other cyclists say about the touring routes in Lagunilla Del Jubera?
A: The touring cycling routes in Lagunilla Del Jubera are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from 3 reviews. Over 160 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the varied terrain and scenic views.

Q: Can I combine cycling with other activities like wine tasting or cultural visits?
A: Absolutely. La Rioja is renowned for its wine. Many routes in the wider region combine cycling with gastronomic and wine-tasting experiences, passing through lush vineyards and traditional wineries. The area also offers access to historic monasteries like San Millán de la Cogolla and medieval towns such as Laguardia, providing a rich cultural immersion alongside your ride.

Q: Where can I find parking for my bike near the routes?
A: While specific parking areas for cyclists are not detailed, Lagunilla Del Jubera and nearby towns like Ribafrecha, Lagunilla de Jubera, and Santa Engracia del Jubera serve as starting points for many routes. It's advisable to check local amenities in these villages for suitable parking options before your ride.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the cycling routes with a bike?
A: Specific public transport options for bikes directly to Lagunilla Del Jubera are not widely detailed. However, Logroño, the capital of La Rioja, is a major hub with more extensive public transport connections. From there, you might find local services or consider driving to a starting point in the Lagunilla Del Jubera area.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay near the cycling routes?
A: Lagunilla Del Jubera and its surrounding villages offer local amenities. While specific cafes, pubs, or accommodations are not listed, you can expect to find options for refreshments and overnight stays in the main towns and villages along or near the routes, such as Lagunilla Del Jubera itself, Ribafrecha, or Santa Engracia del Jubera.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for cycling in La Rioja?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for touring cycling on public roads and established trails in La Rioja. However, if you plan to venture into protected natural areas or private land, it's always best to check local regulations or signage for any restrictions or permit requirements.
tips: Romanesque-style hermitage located one kilometer from Leza de Río Leza, a small picturesque town in Camero Viejo. The first documents found on this magnificent hermitage date from the year 1081, although the current building dates from the 12th century. It is a masonry and masonry construction that is in a perfect state of conservation.
Hermitage of Baroque construction from the 17th century with elements from the 16th century. Among its elements, a small Baroque altarpiece and a dome with a lantern on semicircular arches stand out.

Lagunilla del Jubera is a municipality located on the slopes of Camero Viejo, between the Zenzano mountain range and the Jubera valley. The town is located at an altitude of 612 meters and is crossed by the Salado River.
